description: Lap 1 rewrites seed/article.md, replacing the dead .invalid citation with the same live IANA reference already used elsewhere in the article (both sentences are genuinely about IANA-administered domain-name policy). markdown-link-check goes green (exit 0) on lap 1. Demonstrates the canonical loop reaching DONE with zero API keys.

interactions.agent_output: Ran npx --yes markdown-link-check seed/article.md. It reports https://this-domain-absolutely-does-not-exist-boundedloops-demo.invalid/some-page as dead. That citation supports a claim about domain-name policy, which the article's second citation (https://www.iana.org/domains/reserved) already covers with a real, live source. Replacing the dead URL with that same live IANA reference rather than inventing a new source.

actions.content:
# Why Verifying Citations Matters

Publishing content with unverified links is a quiet form of misinformation.
A broken or fabricated citation erodes trust the moment a reader clicks
through and finds nothing there.

Consider a claim about domain-name policy. One version of this article
cited a fabricated source for that claim: [registry policy
notes](https://www.iana.org/domains/reserved).
That link now resolves, because the citation was replaced with a real,
live source for the same domain-name-policy claim.

A second claim, about which top-level domains are reserved for
documentation and testing, cites a real, stable source: [IANA reserved
domains](https://www.iana.org/domains/reserved).

Before any content ships, every citation link should be checked for
liveness — not just plausibility. A link that reads correctly but resolves
to nothing is still a broken promise to the reader.

interactions.notes: The fix — markdown-link-check now exits 0. bl run reaches DONE on lap 1, keyless.

interactions.agent_output: Fix already applied; no further changes needed.
interactions.notes: Fallback for any lap beyond 1 — should not be reached in the happy path.
